info_gaps: any recent illness, travel, or sick contacts

resource.monitored_bed: 1 of 4 free — should be assigned to this patient

time.door_to_provider: patient arrived 10:48; needs MD eval now given vital derangement

warnings: Patient appearance (well, joking, wants to leave) is DISCORDANT with vitals — do not be falsely reassured
Meets SIRS/sepsis screen criteria: T 38.7, HR 142, RR 26, SBP 88 — 4 of 5 criteria positive
Hypotension + tachycardia + hypoxia + fever = shock physiology until proven otherwise
Young patient with high physiologic reserve can compensate then crash suddenly

hypotheses.name: Occult hemorrhage / hypovolemia (e.g., ruptured ectopic, intra-abdominal bleed post-exertion)
rules_against: fever 38.7 not typical for pure hemorrhage

hypotheses.name: Pulmonary embolism
rules_against: no pleuritic pain or leg swelling reported

hypotheses.name: Rhabdomyolysis with sepsis-like picture
rules_against: no muscle pain or dark urine reported

immediate.action: Place in monitored bed NOW, supplemental O2, continuous cardiac monitor, two large-bore IVs, draw sepsis labs (CBC, BMP, lactate, blood cultures x2, UA, β-hCG, coags, VBG)
immediate.reason: Meets sepsis screen with hypotension; SpO2 91%; needs full resuscitation workup before clinical deterioration.

immediate.action: Begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus (≈2L NS/LR) for hypotension; reassess BP/HR after each 500 mL
immediate.reason: SBP 88 with HR 142 — sepsis bundle and shock resuscitation; address hypovolemia regardless of etiology.

immediate.action: MD bedside evaluation now; if infection source identified or lactate ≥2, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h of recognition
immediate.reason: Sepsis bundle 1-hour antibiotic target; physician must determine source and weigh occult hemorrhage (hold abx until β-hCG/exam).

monitor.action: Vitals q5min until stable, then q15min; trend lactate at 0 and 2h; strict I/O; pulse ox continuous
monitor.reason: Shock physiology in a young compensator — deterioration can be abrupt.

monitor.action: Bedside FAST/RUSH ultrasound + CXR; consider CT abdomen/pelvis once stabilized to evaluate occult hemorrhage, intra-abdominal source, PE
monitor.reason: Differentiate sepsis vs hemorrhage vs PE; β-hCG result gates contrast/radiation decisions.

escalate_or_redirect.action: Notify charge nurse + on-call MD; consider ICU/critical care consult early given shock vitals in a young patient
escalate_or_redirect.reason: Single MD on shift; shock patient will consume disproportionate resources and may need admission to higher level of care.

explanation.summary: 28F who looks well but vitals show septic-shock physiology: T 38.7, HR 142, SBP 88/54, SpO2 91%, RR 26. Meets sepsis screen (R-SEPSIS-01) with hypotension (R-VITALS-HYPOTN) and hypoxia (R-VITALS-HYPOXIA). Despite reassuring appearance and patient's wish to leave, this is ESI 1–2 (R-ESI-T2 minimum, likely R-ESI-T1) — DO NOT discharge. Differential also includes occult hemorrhage (ectopic in 28F) and PE. Resuscitate, search for source, β-hCG before antibiotics/imaging.

citations.signal: T 38.7, HR 142, RR 26, SBP 88 — meets sepsis screen with hypotension
high-risk situation with concerning vitals; minimum ESI 2, likely ESI 1
shock physiology may require vasoactive support and aggressive resuscitation

checklist.task: Assign monitored bed and place on continuous cardiac/SpO2 monitor
Two large-bore IVs, draw sepsis labs including lactate, blood cultures x2, β-hCG
MD bedside evaluation, source assessment, decide on empiric antibiotics within 1h
Repeat vitals q5min until stable; reassess after each fluid bolus
Document patient's stated wish to leave + capacity assessment; do NOT allow AMA without MD discussion given unstable vitals
Early ICU/critical care consult if persistent hypotension after 30 mL/kg or lactate ≥4

why_this_tier: Four concurrent vital red flags: hypotension, tachycardia, hypoxia, fever
Patient's well appearance is a known trap in young compensators with shock
Conservative bias: when vitals and gestalt disagree in a young patient, trust vitals
would_downgrade_if: Repeat vitals on a different cuff/machine show normalization (artifact)
Rapid resolution after 500 mL bolus + clear benign explanation (e.g., post-exertion + accurate rewarming reveals normothermia)
Lactate normal, β-hCG negative, no infection source, and full vital normalization with rest/fluids
would_upgrade_if: Lactate ≥4, persistent SBP <90 after 30 mL/kg, altered mental status, or any sign of end-organ hypoperfusion → ESI 1 / vasopressors
Positive β-hCG with peritoneal signs → emergent surgical/OB consult for ruptured ectopic

similar_cases.summary: 28F | chief complaint: Motor vehicle collision | vitals: HR 132 BP 78/48 SpO2 92 RR 32 Temp 97.8 Pain 10 | nurse note: Unrestrained driver, prolonged extrication, GCS 13, pelvis unstable on exam. EMS reports significant dashboard intrusion. | history: High-speed MVC, airbag deployed, steering wheel

56M | chief complaint: Chest pain | vitals: HR 102 BP 156/88 SpO2 97 RR 20 Temp 98.6 Pain 7 | nurse note: Anxious appearing, substernal pressure for 3 hours, intermittent. Patient has cardiac history with prior stent. | history: CAD with stent 2 years ago, DM, current smoker. Atypical but concerning

26F | chief complaint: Headache | vitals: HR 102 BP 188/118 SpO2 98 RR 18 Temp 98.9 Pain 8 | nurse note: Alert, severe pounding headache, reports palpitations and sweating episodes. Patient appears flushed, hands tremulous. | history: Episodic severe headaches with sweating and palpitations for 3 mo
